planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich, fast-draining, slightly acidic to neutral, organic-rich growing medium in part shade; Tahitian vanilla (V. tahitensis) is a distinct species from Mexican vanilla (V. planifolia, already well-known) — producing plumper, thicker, more aromatic beans with a distinctly different, more floral, anise-like, cherry-heliotropin flavor profile preferred by many gourmet pastry chefs; requires hand-pollination and a tree, trellis, or post to climb.

directions: Root stem cuttings containing 3–4 nodes in moist, organic, fast-draining medium in part shade; hand-pollinate the flowers as they open; harvest beans after 9 months on vine when tips turn yellow. Vanilla tahitensis (Tahitian vanilla) is a separate species from the dominant commercial vanilla (Vanilla planifolia) — distinguished botanically and aromatically by several key differences: the beans are shorter, plumper, and contain more moisture; the primary aromatic compound is heliotropin (piperonal) — a floral, anise-cherry compound — rather than the vanillin-dominated aroma of V. planifolia; and the overall scent profile of Tahitian vanilla beans is more complex, fruity, and intensely floral, with distinctive cherry, anise, and licorice notes absent from Mexican or Madagascar vanilla. These aromatic differences have made Tahitian vanilla the preferred choice of French pastry chefs and many gourmet bakers for delicate preparations where the distinctive floral complexity can shine — French vanilla ice cream, pastry creams, and fine chocolates. Tahitian vanilla is grown primarily in Tahiti and French Polynesia, with smaller production in Papua New Guinea. Like V. planifolia, V. tahitensis requires hand-pollination in cultivation outside its native range, as the natural pollinators (specific bees and hummingbirds) are absent. The vine climbs strongly on trees or supports in humid tropical conditions.
sources: University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Vanilla Production
Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry — Vanilla tahitensis Flavor Chemistry
Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), fertile, loamy soil in part shade; Liberica coffee is the rare third major commercial coffee species — larger in all respects than arabica or robusta (larger tree, larger leaves, larger fruits, larger beans) — with an unusual, divisive flavor: strongly fruity, somewhat smoky, with distinctive jackfruit, dark chocolate, and floral notes that make it controversial but passionately loved by some specialty coffee drinkers; the national coffee of the Philippines (barako).

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile, loamy soil in part shade; Liberica coffee grows as a larger tree than arabica and needs more space; tolerates slightly drier conditions than arabica. Coffea liberica (Liberica coffee or barako) is the rare third major commercial coffee species — a larger, bolder, more assertive plant than arabica (C. arabica) or robusta (C. canephora). Native to the lowland forests of Liberia and West Africa, C. liberica is a substantial tree reaching 20–35 feet (much taller than the 10–15 foot arabica shrub), with large, glossy leaves, large white flowers, and large, asymmetric, elongated coffee fruits (cherries) containing unusually shaped, irregular beans. The flavor of Liberica coffee is perhaps the most controversial in the coffee world: strongly fruity with jackfruit-like, slightly funky, floral-aromatic, sometimes smoky notes — entirely different from the clean, nuanced arabica profile or the bold-bitter robusta profile. Some specialty coffee drinkers consider Liberica's complex, funky, tropical fruit character uniquely wonderful; others find it off-putting. Liberica coffee is of particular importance in the Philippines, where it is known as barako (from a Tagalog word for a virile bull) and has strong cultural identity as the traditional Filipino coffee — once produced in significant quantities on Luzon until coffee rust devastated plantations in the 1880s. Today barako is a prized specialty product in the Philippines.
sources: Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 5.0–7.0), fertile, deep soil in part shade; pataste (Theobroma bicolor) is the 'other cacao' — a close relative of chocolate cacao (T. cacao) native to Central America and the Amazon, cultivated since pre-Columbian times as a chocolate substitute and flavoring; the seeds can be fermented like cacao, the white pulp around the seeds is sweet and edible, and the species was widely used by the Maya and Aztec as a flavoring for chocolate drinks.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile, deep soil in part shade; pataste grows naturally as an understory tree and requires shade protection, especially when young. Theobroma bicolor (pataste, mocambo cacao, or two-colored cacao) is a close relative of chocolate cacao (T. cacao) — one of the approximately 20 edible Theobroma species native to the tropical Americas — and one of the most historically significant secondary cacao species. Like T. cacao, it is native to the humid forests of Mesoamerica and the Amazon basin and produces large, ribbed, gourd-like fruits containing seeds embedded in white, sweet, edible pulp. The seeds of T. bicolor were used by the pre-Columbian Maya and Aztec as a cacao substitute and flavoring in chocolate beverages — often blended with T. cacao to modify flavor, reduce cost, or add specific aromatic notes. The seeds can be fermented and processed similarly to cacao beans to produce a chocolate-like paste and beverage. The white, sweet, mucilaginous fruit pulp surrounding the seeds (similar to the cacao pulp used in Colombia and Ecuador) is pleasantly sweet-acid and is eaten fresh or processed into juice, ice cream, and fermented beverages. T. bicolor was once more widely cultivated than it is today; it is currently grown in home gardens and small farms across Guatemala, Honduras, and Mexico, but has been largely displaced by T. cacao in commercial production. A fascinating collector's tree connecting to chocolate's deep pre-Columbian history.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops — Theobroma bicolor
Kew Royal Botanic Gardens — Plants of the World Online

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep soil in full sun to part shade; African wild mango (ogbono) is a large West African forest tree producing mango-like fruits with sweet, edible pulp — but valued primarily for the large, hard-shelled seeds (ogbono or dika nuts), which are dried and ground into ogbono powder, the critical thickening and flavoring agent in Nigeria's beloved ogbono soup and other West African forest-zone soups.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep soil in full sun to part shade; African wild mango grows as a large forest tree requiring considerable space in zone 10–12. Irvingia gabonensis (African wild mango, ogbono, or dika nut) is a large, canopy forest tree native to the humid tropical forests of West and Central Africa — particularly Nigeria, Cameroon, Gabon, Ivory Coast, and the Congo Basin — and one of the most economically important non-timber forest trees of the West African rainforest zone. The tree produces round to oval, 2–3 inch fruits that superficially resemble small mangoes externally (hence 'African wild mango') but are unrelated: the fruit flesh (mesocarp) is sweet, yellowish, edible, and pleasant, but it is the large, hard-shelled seeds inside that represent the primary economic and culinary product. The seeds (ogbono nuts or dika nuts) are dried, smoked, or processed into ground ogbono powder — the essential thickening and flavoring agent in Nigerian ogbono soup (also called draw soup for its slimy, mucilaginous texture) and related soups across the forest zone of West Africa. Ground ogbono has a distinctive, complex, slightly bitter, earthy-nutty flavor and contains mucilaginous compounds (similar to okra) that create the characteristic 'draw' texture of the soup. The seeds also yield dika oil (Gaboon chocolate), used in confectionery. The tree gained international attention as a weight-loss supplement ingredient (IGOB131 extract) in the 2000s, with preliminary studies suggesting effects on blood lipids.
sources: FAO — Irvingia gabonensis: A Multipurpose African Tree
ICRAF World Agroforestry Centre — Irvingia gabonensis
Purdue University Center for New Crops — African Wild Mango

common_name: African Walnut (Gaboon Nut, Walnut of the Congo)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep soil in part shade; African walnut is a large West African rainforest tree producing olive-to-cherry-sized fruits with a hard shell enclosing a single, walnut-flavored edible kernel — one of the most important wild nut foods of the rainforest zone of Cameroon, Gabon, Nigeria, and the Congo Basin — eaten fresh, roasted, or boiled by forest-dwelling communities.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep soil in part shade; African walnut is a slow-growing canopy tree for large properties in zone 11–12 humid tropics. Coula edulis (African walnut or Gaboon nut) is a large, slow-growing canopy tree of the humid tropical rainforests of West and Central Africa — native to Cameroon, Gabon, Equatorial Guinea, Congo, and the DRC — and one of the most important wild nut-producing trees of the Central African forest zone. The tree produces small (1–1.5 inch), olive-to-cherry-sized, oval to round fruits with a thin, olive-like outer flesh and an extremely hard, woody shell inside (in the Olacaceae family, related to the African walnut group). The single kernel within the hard shell has a distinctly walnut-like flavor — nutty, oily, and rich — and is eaten fresh (raw), roasted in hot ashes, or boiled by forest communities across its range. The nuts are gathered from wild trees and are sold in markets in Cameroon, Gabon, and DRC as an important forest food product and source of income for forest-dependent communities. The high fat content (40–50%) makes the kernels calorie-dense and nutritionally valuable. Coula edulis is listed as a species of conservation concern in parts of its range due to overharvesting of wild nuts and habitat loss. A remarkable and entirely unknown tree outside West Central Africa.
sources: FAO Non-Wood Forest Products — Coula edulis
PROTA — Plant Resources of Tropical Africa
ICRAF World Agroforestry Centre — Central African Edible Forest Products

common_name: Woolly Congea (Shower Orchid Vine, Lilac Vine)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; woolly congea is a spectacularly beautiful tropical vine from Thailand and Southeast Asia — the ornamental display consists of large clusters of pale-lavender to white, woolly, three-lobed bracts (petal-like modified leaves) that cover the vine in winter and early spring in a display sometimes compared to seeing falling lavender snow; actual flowers are tiny, hidden within the dramatic bracts.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; woolly congea is a vigorous vine requiring strong support and can become very large — provide a sturdy pergola or large fence. Congea tomentosa (woolly congea, shower orchid vine, or lilac vine) is one of the most spectacular and unusual tropical flowering vines available for zone 10–12 gardens — a native of Thailand, Myanmar, and Southeast Asian dry tropical forests where it scrambles over trees in the deciduous forest canopy. The ornamental display of congea is completely different from most flowering vines: the actual flowers are tiny and inconspicuous, but each small cluster is surrounded by three large, petal-like, woolly bracts (modified leaves) that are the true ornamental display. These bracts, 1–2 inches long, are produced in enormous quantities in dense, cascading clusters that cover the vine during the cool, dry season (winter to early spring in zone 10–12) — pale lavender-lilac when fresh, fading to silvery-white as they age. The overall effect of a flowering congea is one of cascading pale-lavender to white woolly clusters hanging in billowing masses from the vine — sometimes compared to a waterfall of lavender or a shower of lilac snow. The vine can reach enormous size (30–50 feet) on a large pergola or tree. After flowering, the silvery dried bracts persist attractively for weeks. A breathtaking and rare ornamental vine for large tropical gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Flowering Vines
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Rare Tropical Vines
